epigraph.text: For every complex problem, there is a solution that is simple, neat, and wrong.

follyOfTheDay.headline: Arizona Man Submits Monopoly Money for Mortgage, Calls It 'Performance Art'
follyOfTheDay.summary:
A Phoenix-area resident, seized by what can only be described as a baroque misunderstanding of currency mechanics, submitted a stack of Monopoly notes—complete with their cheerful pastel aesthetics and cartoon imagery—to his bank as payment on his mortgage. When questioned about this creative interpretation of financial obligation, the gentleman explained that the Federal Reserve was 'too mainstream' and that board game currency possessed superior 'vibes.' The bank, unmoved by this metaphysical argument, has since terminated his accounts and contacted federal authorities. The incident raises important questions about the precise threshold at which confidence becomes delusion, and whether whimsy is a legitimate substitute for solvency.

What makes this story transcendent is not merely its surface absurdity, but the underlying logic: a complete inversion of cause and effect, wherein the problem (insufficient funds) was solved not through labor or adjustment of expectations, but through the application of items from a closet shelf. It represents a triumph of creative thinking over practical reality—the very essence of modern American problem-solving.
follyOfTheDay.commentary: At least he attempted payment. Most would have simply declared themselves too 'emotionally exhausted' to honor their debts.
